plainMeaning: This verse bows to still more holy towns — Vaḍnagar, Ḍūṅgarpur, Iḍar and the whole Malwa country — places honoured because the great auspicious events of the Jinas' lives are remembered there.
recitation: vaḍanagara ne ḍūṅgarapure, sā॰ ॥ iḍara mālavadeśa. kalyāṇaka jihāṁ jinataṇāṁ, sā॰

wordByWord.gloss: Vaḍnagar (an ancient temple-town of north Gujarat)
wordByWord.etymology: vaṭa (banyan) + nagara (city), 'banyan-city'; the old Ānandapura / Vṛddhanagara
wordByWord.notes: Object of veneration in the tīrthamālā; a recognised Śvetāmbara Jina-temple site.

wordByWord.etymology: old Gujarati connective particle 'ne' linking the two place-names
wordByWord.notes: Here a coordinating 'and', not the modern accusative/dative marker.
Locative case; town in southern Rajasthan with a noted Ādinātha temple.

wordByWord.etymology: a dhruvapada (recurring burden) abbreviated by the scribal circle '૦'; the full refrain-word is not spelled out
wordByWord.notes: Repeated at each half-line as the sung refrain; printed verbatim as an abbreviation in the book.

wordByWord.etymology: older Iḍara / Iḍaranagara; cf. the old names Illaspadra, Illadurga
wordByWord.notes: Sabarkantha hill-town; long associated with Śāntinātha and Ādīśvara images.
Names a whole region rather than a single town, widening the sweep.

wordByWord.etymology: from kalyāṇa (auspicious, welfare, well-being) + suffix -ka; the set of sacred Tīrthaṅkara events
wordByWord.notes: Traditionally the five kalyāṇakas: conception, birth, renunciation, omniscience, liberation.

wordByWord.etymology: old Gujarati relative locative adverb 'where' (cf. Skt. yatra, Prakrit jahiṁ)
wordByWord.notes: Relative adverb introducing the reason these places are holy.

wordByWord.etymology: jina (Jina) + taṇāṁ, the old Gujarati genitive postposition 'of' (later → -nāṁ)
wordByWord.notes: Plural genitive agreeing with kalyāṇaka; 'the Jinas'' auspicious events'.

wordByWord.gloss: [abbreviated refrain / burden of the dhāla, repeated]
wordByWord.etymology: second occurrence of the same abbreviated dhruvapada
wordByWord.notes: Closes the verse with the same sung burden.

literalTranslation: At Vaḍnagar and at Ḍūṅgarpur — [refrain] — Iḍar, the Malwa country; where there are the auspicious events (kalyāṇakas) of the Jinas — [refrain].
idiomaticTranslation: To Vaḍnagar and to Ḍūṅgarpur I bow — and to Iḍar, and across the whole Malwa land — every place where the sacred kalyāṇakas of the Jinas are kept in honour.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: The garland of holy places turns inland. The previous verse had ranged over coastal and Deccan sites — Devakapāṭaṇ, Devagiri, Navānagar, the harbour of Dīv; this verse gathers the north-Gujarat and Rajasthan interior — Vaḍnagar, Ḍūṅgarpur, Iḍar — and then, with mālavadeśa, swells from single towns to a whole region, Malwa. The widening from town to country is deliberate: the reciter's bow grows larger with each breath. The verse's hinge is its second line, kalyāṇaka jihāṁ jinataṇāṁ — 'where the auspicious events of the Jinas are.' This is the one word that turns a mere list into theology: a town is named not for its trade or its kings but because the Jinas' kalyāṇakas are remembered and celebrated there through their enshrined images and consecrated temples. The relative jihāṁ ('where') thus supplies the reason for every name in the dhāla, not only these. The verse then flows straight into the following one — gām nagar pur pāṭaṇ ('village, town, city, capital') — where the poet finally generalises: wherever a Jina-image stands, that place too is bowed to, and Vācaka Mūlā signs off. So this verse stands just before the poet drops the particular names and embraces all places at once.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The operative doctrine is kalyāṇaka-bhakti. A kalyāṇaka is one of the five supremely auspicious turning-points in a Tīrthaṅkara's career, and the Jain conviction is that place itself is sanctified by contact with such events and with the Jina's enshrined presence — a tīrtha is literally a 'ford' across saṁsāra. By saying these towns are where 'the kalyāṇakas of the Jinas' are, the verse extends kalyāṇaka-reverence from the original sacred geography to living centres of image-worship (caitya-vandana through pratimā), the very act this whole hymn performs.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting this line, the worshipper makes a mental pilgrimage across Gujarat, Rajasthan and Malwa without leaving the dawn posture — naming each town is an interior circumambulation. It trains the practitioner to see ordinary towns as potential tīrthas and to greet any local derāsar as a kalyāṇaka-site worthy of a bow.

crossReferences.text: The five kalyāṇakas (garbha, janma, dīkṣā, kevala-jñāna, nirvāṇa) of each Tīrthaṅkara
crossReferences.source: Jain dogmatics; cf. Tattvārtha tradition and standard pratiṣṭhā-kalpa

crossReferences.text: Aṣṭottarī Tīrthamālā (108 tīrthas, by Mahendrasinhasūri), the Pakkhī tīrtha-litany
crossReferences.source: Anchalgaccha (Vidhipaksha) Panch Pratikraman Sūtra – Vidhi Sahit

sources.title: Kalyāṇaka — definition
sources.consultedFor: Sense of kalyāṇaka as an auspicious Tīrthaṅkara life-event and the set of five.

sources.title: Tirtha (Jainism)
sources.consultedFor: Concept of a tīrtha as a sanctified 'ford' and the logic of place-veneration.

sources.title: Shri Vadnagar Tirth — Jain Universe
sources.consultedFor: Identity and antiquity of Vaḍnagar as a Śvetāmbara Jina-temple site.

sources.title: Idar Tirth — The Jainsite
sources.consultedFor: Identity of Iḍar as a hill-fortress tīrth with Śāntinātha/Ādīśvara images and its old names.

notes: The refrain 'સા૦' is printed as an abbreviation (the Gujarati circle '૦' marking an elided dhruvapada); the full burden-word is not spelled out in the verse, so it is rendered only as '[refrain]' and its exact form is left uncertain rather than guessed. Reading of 'kalyāṇaka jihāṁ jinataṇāṁ': the named towns are not the historical kalyāṇaka-bhūmis of this avasarpiṇī's twenty-four Jinas (which lie at Ayodhyā, Sammeta-śikhara, Pāvāpurī, etc.); the line is best taken devotionally — places 'where the Jinas' kalyāṇakas are [honoured/celebrated]' through enshrined images — and this interpretive nuance is flagged rather than asserted as literal sacred geography. Spellings (વડનગર, ડુંગરપુરે, જિહાં, જિનતણાં, માલવદેશ) and the spaced bar/daṇḍa punctuation are kept verbatim per the book's orthography. Place identifications are corroborated but not exhaustively audited.
